subtopic_detail: TCA overdose with QRS widening management
stem: You are the ED registrar at a metropolitan hospital seeing a 28-year-old man brought in 90 minutes after ingestion of about 40 amitriptyline 25 mg tablets in a suicide attempt. He is drowsy but responsive, with dry mouth and dilated pupils. His ECG shows sinus tachycardia with a QRS of 140 ms and right axis deviation of the terminal R wave in aVR. He has no seizures and a single dose of activated charcoal has been administered. Toxicology has been notified.

data_table.Vital signs: GCS 13, HR 124, BP 96/58, RR 18, SpO2 98% room air, T 36.7 °C, BSL 6.2
data_table.Examination: Drowsy but rousable, dry mucous membranes, mydriasis, no focal neurological deficit, no clonus
data_table.Investigations: ECG: sinus tachycardia, QRS 140 ms, terminal R in aVR >3 mm; venous blood gas pH 7.30, lactate 3.2 mmol/L, K 3.6 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next intervention?

options.text: Administer intravenous sodium bicarbonate 1 to 2 mmol/kg bolus and reassess QRS width and pH
options.rationale: QRS above 100 ms with terminal R wave in aVR signifies sodium channel blockade in TCA toxicity; sodium bicarbonate 1 to 2 mmol/kg bolus reverses the conduction abnormality. (Source: NSW Poisons Information)

options.text: Commence intravenous lipid emulsion 1.5 mL/kg as a first-line antidote prior to alkalinisation therapy
options.rationale: Lipid emulsion is reserved for cardiovascular collapse refractory to bicarbonate in lipophilic drug toxicity; sodium bicarbonate is first-line for TCA QRS widening. (Source: NSW Poisons Information)

options.text: Give intravenous flumazenil 0.2 mg titrated until alert as the immediate next reversal step today
options.rationale: Flumazenil reverses benzodiazepines and has no role in TCA overdose; it can precipitate seizures in mixed ingestions. (Source: NSW Poisons Information)

options.text: Begin intravenous magnesium sulfate 2 g over 10 minutes as the recommended sodium channel antidote
options.rationale: Magnesium is an adjunct for torsades de pointes and refractory dysrhythmia, not the primary therapy for TCA-induced sodium channel blockade. (Source: NSW Poisons Information)

options.text: Commence intravenous procainamide 17 mg/kg to control sinus tachycardia and prevent further conduction loss
options.rationale: Class 1a antiarrhythmics are sodium channel blockers themselves and will worsen TCA cardiotoxicity; they are contraindicated. (Source: NSW Poisons Information)

explanation.summary: Tricyclic antidepressant overdose causes sodium channel blockade, anticholinergic toxicity, alpha-adrenergic blockade and seizures. A QRS above 100 ms predicts seizures and ventricular arrhythmia; a terminal R in aVR above 3 mm is a sensitive marker. Sodium bicarbonate boluses of 1 to 2 mmol/kg narrow the QRS and reverse hypotension. Aim for arterial pH 7.50 to 7.55 and sodium 145 to 155 mmol/L. Lipid emulsion is rescue therapy for collapse not responding to bicarbonate, with toxicology input.
key_points: QRS above 100 ms drives bicarbonate therapy decision.
Aim for pH 7.50 to 7.55 and sodium 145 to 155 mmol/L.
Avoid class Ia, Ic and III antiarrhythmics.
Lipid emulsion is reserved for refractory collapse.
explanation.pearls: Seizures cause profound metabolic acidosis that worsens TCA cardiotoxicity within minutes; treat with benzodiazepines first-line and avoid phenytoin which is also a sodium channel blocker and may precipitate ventricular tachycardia.

subtopic_detail: Discrimination of NMS from serotonin syndrome with hyperthermia
stem: You are the ED registrar reviewing a 24-year-old man with treatment-resistant depression on phenelzine. He took two doses of tramadol from his mother's medication this evening. Two hours later he is agitated, sweating, with hyperthermia, hypertension, ocular clonus, hyperreflexia and inducible lower limb clonus. His muscles are not lead-pipe rigid and his mental status fluctuates with marked agitation. No antipsychotic exposure. CK is 220 IU/L. Toxicology has been contacted and ICU is aware.

data_table.Vital signs: T 39.6 °C, HR 132, BP 168/102, RR 24, SpO2 98% room air, BSL 6.4
data_table.Examination: Agitated, mydriasis, hyperreflexia greater in lower limbs, sustained clonus at ankles, ocular clonus, sweating
data_table.Investigations: CK 220 IU/L, lactate 3.6 mmol/L, creatinine 92, sodium 138, ECG sinus tachycardia

lead_in: What is the most appropriate specific therapy in addition to supportive care?

options.text: Give intramuscular adrenaline 10 micrograms/kg and intravenous methylprednisolone for autonomic crisis
options.rationale: Adrenaline and steroids have no role and may worsen hypertension and hyperthermia; benzodiazepines plus cyproheptadine are first line. (Source: NSW Poisons Information)

options.text: Start oral bromocriptine 2.5 mg three times daily and intravenous dantrolene for muscle rigidity now
options.rationale: Bromocriptine and dantrolene are reserved for neuroleptic malignant syndrome which features lead-pipe rigidity and bradyreflexia; this presentation is serotonin syndrome. (Source: NSW Poisons Information)

options.text: Administer intravenous haloperidol 5 mg for control of agitation and continue all current medications
options.rationale: Antipsychotics worsen autonomic instability and can precipitate NMS; agitation in serotonin syndrome is treated with benzodiazepines and removal of serotonergic agents. (Source: NSW Poisons Information)

options.text: Restart phenelzine immediately and add oral mirtazapine to block serotonin syndrome symptoms tomorrow
options.rationale: Mirtazapine has 5HT2A blocking properties but should never be restarted with phenelzine; both serotonergic drugs must be ceased. (Source: NSW Poisons Information)

options.text: Cease serotonergic drugs, give midazolam titrated and oral cyproheptadine 12 mg loading dose now
options.rationale: The Hunter criteria pattern of inducible clonus, ocular clonus and hyperreflexia fit serotonin syndrome; benzodiazepines and the 5HT2A antagonist cyproheptadine are the specific Australian-recommended therapy. (Source: NSW Poisons Information)

explanation.summary: Serotonin syndrome from MAOI plus tramadol is a clinical diagnosis using Hunter criteria: spontaneous or inducible clonus, ocular clonus, agitation, hyperreflexia, hypertension and hyperthermia. NMS instead features lead-pipe rigidity, bradyreflexia and slow onset after antipsychotic dose escalation. Treatment is removal of serotonergic agents, intravenous fluid, cooling, benzodiazepines for agitation and rigidity, and cyproheptadine if symptoms persist. Severe hyperthermia above 41 °C warrants intubation, paralysis and active cooling in ICU.
key_points: Clonus and hyperreflexia favour serotonin syndrome over NMS.
Benzodiazepines plus cyproheptadine are specific therapy.
Avoid antipsychotics in serotonin syndrome.
Intubate and paralyse if T above 41 °C with rigidity.
explanation.pearls: Cyproheptadine loading is 12 mg orally, then 2 mg every 2 hours up to 32 mg in 24 hours; if the patient cannot swallow, crush and give via nasogastric tube because there is no parenteral preparation in Australia.

subtopic_detail: TIA ABCD2 risk stratification and admission threshold
stem: You are the ED registrar reviewing a 72-year-old man with a 25-minute episode of right-sided weakness and dysarthria that resolved 1 hour ago. He has hypertension, diabetes and is in sinus rhythm. CT brain is normal. He has no atrial fibrillation on telemetry. He is on perindopril, metformin and atorvastatin. Carotid Doppler ultrasound is available the next morning at the same site. He has no contraindication to antiplatelet therapy.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 162/92, HR 78, RR 16, T 36.5 °C, SpO2 98% room air, BSL 8.2
data_table.Examination: Normal neurological examination now, no carotid bruit, no facial droop, no dysarthria currently
data_table.Investigations: CT brain unremarkable, ECG sinus rhythm, troponin negative; carotid Doppler tomorrow morning

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management plan?

options.text: Admit for inpatient stroke workup, start aspirin 300 mg today and arrange carotid imaging today
options.rationale: ABCD2 score 6 is high-risk for stroke within 48 hours; admit and complete carotid imaging and antiplatelet loading the same day to reduce early recurrence. (Source: Stroke Foundation Living Guidelines)

options.text: Discharge home with aspirin 100 mg daily and a routine outpatient carotid Doppler in two weeks time
options.rationale: ABCD2 6 places this patient in the highest-risk group; outpatient management delays urgent revascularisation if a critical stenosis is present. (Source: Stroke Foundation Living Guidelines)

options.text: Commence apixaban 5 mg twice daily today and arrange carotid Doppler tomorrow morning as planned
options.rationale: DOACs are reserved for cardioembolic TIA with atrial fibrillation; he is in sinus rhythm and antiplatelet therapy is appropriate. (Source: Stroke Foundation Living Guidelines)

options.text: Arrange urgent MRI brain with diffusion weighting prior to commencing any antiplatelet therapy today
options.rationale: Antiplatelet should not be delayed to wait for MRI; loading dose of aspirin reduces early recurrence and can be given before further imaging. (Source: Stroke Foundation Living Guidelines)

options.text: Refer to outpatient TIA clinic in 7 days without commencing any new medication today at all
options.rationale: Delaying assessment and antiplatelet therapy by a week in a high-risk TIA misses the window for preventing early stroke recurrence. (Source: Stroke Foundation Living Guidelines)

explanation.summary: ABCD2 score predicts early stroke risk after TIA using age, blood pressure, clinical features, duration and diabetes. Scores of 4 or more, or any TIA with atrial fibrillation, warrant urgent same-day assessment in Australia. Aspirin 300 mg loading then 100 mg daily plus carotid imaging within 24 hours reduces early stroke. Dual antiplatelet with aspirin and clopidogrel for 21 days further reduces recurrence after minor stroke and high-risk TIA. Statin and BP control complete secondary prevention.
key_points: ABCD2 4 or more is high-risk for early stroke recurrence.
Load with aspirin 300 mg then 100 mg daily.
Image carotids and brain within 24 hours.
Use DAPT for 21 days after high-risk TIA or minor stroke.
explanation.pearls: About 5 percent of high-risk TIA patients have an ischaemic stroke within 48 hours; an effective rapid-access TIA clinic combined with same-day antiplatelet loading reduces this risk by approximately 80 percent.

subtopic_detail: AF cardioversion timing and DOAC anticoagulation
stem: You are the ED registrar managing a 64-year-old man with new symptomatic atrial fibrillation. Onset was 18 hours ago by his account during exertion. He has hypertension, diabetes and a CHA2DS2-VA score of 4. He is haemodynamically stable. He has no history of bleeding and no contraindication to anticoagulation. Transthoracic echocardiogram shows normal left ventricular function and no thrombus visualised. He prefers rhythm control if safe and you have decided cardioversion is appropriate.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 138/82, HR 138 irregular, RR 18, SpO2 98% room air, T 36.6 °C
data_table.Examination: Irregularly irregular pulse, no chest pain, no signs of heart failure, no carotid bruit
data_table.Investigations: Hb 142, U&E normal, INR 1.0, ECG atrial fibrillation 132 bpm, TTE LVEF 60% no thrombus, TFTs normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate anticoagulation plan around DC cardioversion?

options.text: Defer cardioversion until 3 weeks of therapeutic apixaban 5 mg twice daily have been completed
options.rationale: Onset under 48 hours allows early cardioversion after pre-procedural anticoagulation; full 3 week pretreatment is for established or uncertain onset AF. (Source: NHFA AF Guidelines)

options.text: Start apixaban 5 mg twice daily now, cardiovert today and continue anticoagulation for at least 4 weeks
options.rationale: AF onset under 48 hours allows early cardioversion after at least one DOAC dose, with at least 4 weeks of anticoagulation afterwards and long-term anticoagulation given high CHA2DS2-VA. (Source: NHFA AF Guidelines)

options.text: Cardiovert immediately without anticoagulation because no thrombus was seen on the transthoracic echo today
options.rationale: Transthoracic echo cannot exclude left atrial appendage thrombus; TOE is required if cardioversion is needed without prior anticoagulation. (Source: NHFA AF Guidelines)

options.text: Bridge with intravenous unfractionated heparin and cardiovert in 7 days after admission to coronary care
options.rationale: Heparin bridging is unnecessary with effective DOAC therapy; modern Australian practice favours single DOAC dose then cardioversion within 48 hour window. (Source: NHFA AF Guidelines)

options.text: Cardiovert without anticoagulation now and start aspirin 100 mg daily for long-term thromboprophylaxis
options.rationale: Aspirin is not recommended for stroke prevention in AF; CHA2DS2-VA 4 needs full anticoagulation with a DOAC or warfarin. (Source: NHFA AF Guidelines)

explanation.summary: For atrial fibrillation of definite onset under 48 hours, cardioversion can proceed after at least one therapeutic DOAC dose. For uncertain or longer-duration AF, either three weeks of effective anticoagulation or transoesophageal echo exclusion of thrombus is required first. Anticoagulation continues for at least four weeks after cardioversion regardless of CHA2DS2-VA score. Long-term anticoagulation depends on CHA2DS2-VA score; 2 or more warrants ongoing DOAC therapy in non-valvular AF.
key_points: Cardiovert under 48 h after a single DOAC dose.
Use TOE if onset uncertain or anticoagulation incomplete.
Continue anticoagulation 4 weeks after cardioversion.
CHA2DS2-VA 2 or more triggers long-term DOAC.
explanation.pearls: About 5 percent of patients have post-cardioversion stunning and silent thromboembolism even after successful restoration of sinus rhythm; four weeks of post-cardioversion anticoagulation reduces this to under 1 percent in non-valvular AF.

subtopic_detail: Hour-1 bundle and vasopressor choice in septic shock
stem: You are the ED registrar reviewing a 58-year-old woman with two days of dysuria, fever and rigors. She is hypotensive with cool peripheries. She has no allergies and no chronic disease. Initial intravenous crystalloid 30 mL/kg has been given over the past hour but her mean arterial pressure remains 58 mmHg. Lactate is 4.6 mmol/L. Urine, blood cultures and a chest X-ray have been completed. Empirical antibiotics for severe urosepsis are being prepared.

data_table.Vital signs: T 39.2 °C, HR 122, BP 86/52, MAP 58, RR 24, SpO2 96% room air, UO 0.3 mL/kg/h
data_table.Examination: Warm flushed centrally, cool peripheries, no focal chest signs, suprapubic tenderness, no rash
data_table.Investigations: Lactate 4.6 mmol/L, CRP 220, creatinine 138, WCC 18 x10^9/L, urinalysis leucocytes and nitrites positive

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in haemodynamic management?

options.text: Continue further intravenous crystalloid up to 50 mL/kg before commencing any vasopressor support
options.rationale: Fluid above 30 mL/kg without response is unlikely to correct vasodilatory hypotension and increases pulmonary oedema risk; start vasopressor now. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign)

options.text: Commence intravenous noradrenaline infusion via large-bore peripheral line targeting MAP 65 mmHg
options.rationale: Septic shock with MAP under 65 after 30 mL/kg fluid requires vasopressor; noradrenaline is first-line and can be initiated peripherally while central access is being secured. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign)

options.text: Administer intravenous adrenaline 1 mg bolus and commence infusion targeting systolic above 100 mmHg
options.rationale: Adrenaline boluses are reserved for cardiac arrest; adrenaline infusion is second line in septic shock when noradrenaline plus vasopressin are insufficient. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign)

options.text: Commence dopamine 5 micrograms/kg/min infusion as the recommended first-line vasopressor in septic shock
options.rationale: Dopamine is no longer recommended first line in septic shock due to arrhythmia risk; noradrenaline is preferred. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign)

options.text: Defer vasopressor and add intravenous hydrocortisone 200 mg per day for refractory hypotension now
options.rationale: Hydrocortisone is added when noradrenaline doses exceed 0.25 micrograms/kg/min, not as a substitute for vasopressor; treatment of hypotension comes first. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign)

explanation.summary: The Surviving Sepsis Campaign hour-1 bundle requires lactate measurement, blood cultures, broad-spectrum antibiotics, 30 mL/kg crystalloid for hypotension or lactate above 4 mmol/L, and vasopressor for ongoing hypotension to keep MAP at least 65 mmHg. Noradrenaline is first-line and can be initiated through a large-bore peripheral cannula while central venous access is arranged. Vasopressin is added when the noradrenaline dose exceeds 0.25 to 0.5 micrograms/kg/min. Hydrocortisone 200 mg per day is added for refractory shock.
key_points: Start vasopressor if MAP under 65 after 30 mL/kg fluid.
Noradrenaline is the first-line agent in septic shock.
Vasopressin is added at noradrenaline 0.25 mcg/kg/min.
Hydrocortisone is reserved for refractory shock.
explanation.pearls: Each hour of antibiotic delay in septic shock increases mortality by approximately 7 percent; in Australian EDs the eMR sepsis pathway aims for antibiotic administration within 60 minutes of triage triggered by qSOFA or screening tool.
